title: Causes of Genetic Variation
subtitle: Fertilisation, meiosis and mutation, three different jobs
subject: Biology
syllabusDotPoints: investigate the causes of genetic variation relating to the processes of fertilisation, meiosis and mutation (ACSBL078)
inquiryQuestion: How does mutation introduce new alleles into a population?
lessonIntent: Students can explain how mutation, meiosis and fertilisation each contribute to variation, separating new alleles from new combinations.
examSkill: Match a described effect to the process responsible and distinguish new alleles from new allele combinations.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Biology. Today, the causes of genetic variation. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Fertilisation, Meiosis and Mutation as Causes of Genetic Variation (Year 12 Biology, Module 6, Lesson 6)

scenes.heading: Deeper beaks in one generation
scenes.body: After a drought, finch beaks got 17% deeper in a single generation, with no new mutations needed.
scenes.callout: How can a population change so fast using only the variation already there?
scenes.caption: Daphne Major finches shifted using pre-existing variation, not new mutation.

voiceover.text: Since nineteen seventy seven, Peter and Rosemary Grant have measured almost every finch on a tiny island called Daphne Major. Then a severe drought hit, and eighty five percent of one finch species died. When the survivors bred, something remarkable happened, the next generation had beaks seventeen percent deeper on average. And here's the twist, no new mutations were needed. That entire shift came from heritable variation that was already present in the population, with a heritability around zero point seven nine. That existing variation had been generated by crossing over, by independent assortment, and by random fertilisation, all acting on alleles already in the gene pool. So today's question is, how do these processes create variation, and where does mutation fit in differently?

scenes.heading: Three sources, three different jobs
scenes.body: Mutation, meiosis and fertilisation all generate variation, but they do completely different jobs.

bullets.text: Mutation: creates new alleles by changing the DNA sequence.
Meiosis: reshuffles existing alleles into genetically different gametes.
Fertilisation: combines gametes randomly into new genotypes.

scenes.secondary: Mutation supplies novelty. Meiosis and fertilisation spread and recombine what is already there. New combination is not new allele.
scenes.callout: Novelty versus reshuffling, keep them apart.
scenes.caption: Mutation makes new alleles; meiosis and fertilisation reshuffle existing ones.

voiceover.text: Let's set up the comparison that the whole lesson hangs on. Three processes generate genetic variation, but they each have a distinct job. Mutation creates genuinely new alleles, by changing the DNA sequence itself. Meiosis reshuffles the alleles that already exist, dealing them out into genetically different gametes. And fertilisation combines those gametes at random, producing new genotypes in the offspring. Notice the division of labour. Mutation is the only one supplying real novelty, brand new alleles. Meiosis and fertilisation, by contrast, spread and recombine what's already there. That's why, after the drought, the finch population could shift so fast, it was reshuffling and selecting existing variation, no new alleles required. So the line to hold on to is simple, a new combination of alleles is not the same thing as a new allele.

scenes.heading: The words you need

bullets.text: Genetic variation: differences in genetic makeup between individuals in a population.
Mutation: a change in DNA sequence that can create a new allele.
Meiosis: cell division producing gametes, reshuffling alleles via assortment and crossing over.
Fertilisation: fusion of two gametes, combining alleles from each parent.
Independent assortment: the random separation of homologous chromosomes in meiosis.
Crossing over: exchange of segments between homologous chromosomes, making recombinants.

scenes.callout: Two meiosis mechanisms: independent assortment and crossing over.
scenes.caption: Genetic variation, mutation, meiosis, fertilisation, independent assortment, crossing over.

voiceover.text: Let's pin down the vocabulary, six terms here. Genetic variation is simply the differences in genetic makeup between individuals in a population. Mutation is a change in DNA sequence that can create a new allele. Meiosis is the cell division that produces gametes, and it reshuffles alleles through two mechanisms. Fertilisation is the fusion of two gametes, combining alleles from each parent into a new offspring. The two meiosis mechanisms are the ones to memorise. Independent assortment is the random separation of homologous chromosomes during meiosis, so different chromosomes end up in different gametes. And crossing over is the exchange of segments between homologous chromosomes, producing recombinant chromatids. Those two, independent assortment and crossing over, are how meiosis generates so much variation without ever making a new allele.

scenes.heading: Meiosis varies gametes before fertilisation
scenes.body: Meiosis generates variation in two ways, before fertilisation even happens, using only existing alleles.

bullets.text: Independent assortment: maternal and paternal chromosomes enter gametes in random mixes.
Crossing over: homologous chromosomes swap segments, making recombinant chromatids.
One parent can make many genetically different gametes with no new mutation.

scenes.secondary: Brothers and sisters differ largely because each parent makes a different set of gametes through meiosis, not because mutation happens every generation.
scenes.callout: Many different gametes, zero new alleles.
scenes.caption: Independent assortment and crossing over vary gametes before fertilisation.

voiceover.text: Let's look closer at meiosis, because it does its work before fertilisation even starts. It contributes to variation in two major ways. First, independent assortment, the homologous chromosomes line up and separate randomly, so different combinations of maternal and paternal chromosomes end up in different gametes. Second, crossing over, where homologous chromosomes physically exchange segments, producing recombinant chromatids that mix maternal and paternal alleles on the same chromosome. Put those together and one parent can produce an enormous number of genetically different gametes, all without a single new mutation. This is exactly why brothers and sisters differ so much, it's mostly because each parent makes a different set of gametes through meiosis, not because a new mutation pops up in the relevant genes every generation. Many different gametes, zero new alleles.

scenes.heading: Fertilisation adds a random layer
scenes.body: Even after meiosis varies the gametes, fertilisation adds more variation because which gametes meet is largely random.

bullets.text: Random fusion combines one maternal and one paternal gamete into a new genotype.
Fertilisation makes new allele combinations, not new alleles.
Selection acts on the whole phenotype these combinations produce.

scenes.secondary: Fertilisation does not create alleles, but it matters biologically because natural selection acts on whole phenotypes, not on isolated alleles.
scenes.callout: New genotypes, still no new alleles.
scenes.caption: Fertilisation randomly combines gametes into new genotypes.

voiceover.text: Now the third layer, fertilisation. Even after meiosis has produced a huge variety of gametes, fertilisation adds yet more variation, because which sperm meets which egg is largely a matter of chance. That random fusion brings together one set of maternal alleles and one set of paternal alleles into a brand new genotype in the offspring. But notice the pattern holding from earlier, fertilisation does not create new alleles by itself, it creates new combinations of alleles that already existed. So why does it matter biologically? Because natural selection acts on whole phenotypes, the integrated outcome of all those combined alleles, not on isolated alleles one at a time. Fertilisation keeps generating fresh genotypes for selection to test. New genotypes, yes, but still no new alleles.

scenes.heading: Match the process
scenes.question: Name the process for each: (a) different maternal and paternal chromosomes enter different gametes; (b) a new allele appears because the DNA sequence changed; (c) one sperm fuses with one egg from many possibilities; (d) homologous chromosomes exchange segments.
scenes.coachNote: Ask: new DNA (mutation), gamete reshuffling (meiosis), or random fusion (fertilisation)?
steps: (a) Random distribution of chromosomes to gametes is meiosis, by independent assortment.
(b) A new allele from a changed DNA sequence is mutation.
(c) Random fusion of one sperm with one egg is fertilisation.
(d) Exchange of segments between homologues is meiosis, by crossing over.
scenes.caption: (a) meiosis, (b) mutation, (c) fertilisation, (d) meiosis.

voiceover.text: Let's match four effects to their process. The test is, did the DNA sequence change, were gametes reshuffled, or were gametes randomly fused? Part a, different maternal and paternal chromosomes enter different gametes. That's chromosomes being distributed randomly, so meiosis, specifically independent assortment. Part b, a new allele appears because the DNA sequence changed. New DNA means mutation, the only process that makes new alleles. Part c, one sperm fuses with one egg out of many possible combinations. That random fusion is fertilisation. Part d, homologous chromosomes exchange segments. That's meiosis again, this time by crossing over, which makes recombinant chromatids. So the answers are meiosis, mutation, fertilisation, meiosis, and notice that meiosis shows up twice because it has two distinct mechanisms.

scenes.heading: The big traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: saying mutation is the only source of genetic variation. Meiosis and fertilisation generate huge variation too, by recombining existing alleles.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: saying meiosis or fertilisation creates new alleles. They only recombine existing ones. Trap 3: using 'variation' as if every source does the same job. Mutation supplies novelty; the others spread combinations.
scenes.callout: All three matter, but each does a different job.
scenes.caption: Mutation is not the only source; meiosis and fertilisation reshuffle, not create.

voiceover.text: Three traps that lose marks. Trap one, saying that mutation is the only source of genetic variation. It isn't. Meiosis and fertilisation generate an enormous amount of variation too, through independent assortment, crossing over, and the random fusion of gametes. Mutation is the source of new alleles, but it is not the source of all variation. Trap two, the mirror image, saying that meiosis or fertilisation creates new alleles. They don't, they only recombine alleles that already exist. And trap three, the lazy one, using the word variation as if every source does the same job. They don't, mutation supplies genuine novelty, while meiosis and fertilisation spread and recombine combinations. A top answer always explains how each process contributes and clearly separates a new allele from a new combination.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: Explain why siblings can differ genetically without any new mutation, then add why mutation still matters in the long term.
answerSteps: Meiosis makes each parent's gametes genetically different, via independent assortment and crossing over.
Fertilisation then combines one gamete from each parent at random.
So each child gets a different combination of existing alleles, no new mutation required.
Mutation still matters because it is the ultimate source of genuinely new alleles.
Answer: reshuffling explains sibling difference; mutation replenishes the variation pool over time.
scenes.caption: Reshuffling explains sibling difference; mutation supplies new alleles long-term.

voiceover.text: Quick check. Explain why siblings can differ genetically without any new mutation, then add why mutation still matters in the long term. Pause and build both parts. Right. Start with the sibling difference. Through meiosis, each parent produces genetically different gametes, using independent assortment of chromosomes and crossing over between them. Then fertilisation combines one gamete from each parent at random. So each child receives a different combination of alleles that already existed in the parents, which means no new mutation is required for siblings to differ. Now the second part. Mutation still matters in the long term because it is the ultimate source of genuinely new alleles, the raw material that replenishes the variation pool once existing variation gets used up. So reshuffling explains why siblings differ today, while mutation keeps the gene pool supplied with novelty over many generations.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: Mutation, meiosis and fertilisation all generate variation, but do different jobs.
Mutation creates new alleles; meiosis and fertilisation reshuffle existing ones.
Meiosis varies gametes via independent assortment and crossing over.
Fertilisation combines gametes randomly into new genotypes.
A new combination of alleles is not the same as a new allele.
scenes.finalPrompt: Asked about variation? Say which process and whether it makes alleles or combinations.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: three jobs, mutation makes alleles, meiosis varies gametes, fertilisation combines.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five takeaways. First, mutation, meiosis and fertilisation all generate genetic variation, but they do different jobs. Second, mutation creates new alleles, while meiosis and fertilisation reshuffle alleles that already exist. Third, meiosis varies the gametes through two mechanisms, independent assortment and crossing over. Fourth, fertilisation combines gametes randomly into new genotypes. And fifth, the headline, a new combination of alleles is not the same as a new allele. So whenever you're asked about variation, say which process is responsible, and be clear about whether it makes new alleles, or just new combinations. See you next lesson.
